headline: Healthcare and Financial-Data Surge as Semiconductors Bear the Session's Brunt
body_html:
Midway through Thursday's abbreviated pre-holiday session, the market is holding a modestly constructive posture — 109 names advancing against 89 declining across a 198-stock universe — though the headline numbers obscure a sharp bifurcation beneath the surface. Healthcare is the clear sector leader, up 3.17%, followed by Consumer names at 1.91% and Homebuilders adding 1.03%. Software is clinging to a slim gain of 0.74%, while Energy sits essentially flat. Industrials and Materials are in the red, down 0.71% and 1.24% respectively.

The dominant theme of the session is an emphatic rotation away from the semiconductor complex. The group is off 6.5% as a sector — the worst showing in the universe by a wide margin — dragged lower by steep declines across memory, test-equipment, packaging, and capital-equipment names. The broader technology narrative is complicated by Apple's 4.84% advance, which is lending some ballast to the hardware space even as chip-related equities crater.

Financial-data names are the stealth story of the morning. Equifax leads the entire universe with a 6.1% intraday gain, with S&P Global close behind at 6.01% and Fair Isaac adding 5.32%. Into the close, traders will be watching whether the semiconductor selling deepens or stabilizes, and whether healthcare's leadership — anchored by Intuitive Surgical's 5.87% advance and Roche's 5.41% move — can hold into a shortened Friday.

briefs.headline: Financial-Data Trio Commands the Leaderboard
briefs.body: Equifax, S&P Global, and Fair Isaac are posting gains of 6.1%, 6.01%, and 5.32% respectively, making Financial-Data the most cohesive winning sector mid-session. The synchronized move suggests a catalyst specific to the group rather than broad risk appetite, and all three remain well above their intraday opening levels.

briefs.headline: Semiconductor Equipment Names Suffer Punishing Selloff
briefs.body: KLA Corp and Entegris are each down more than 11% midday, adding to severe losses in Sandisk, Teradyne, and Amkor Technology — which are off 14.1%, 13.6%, and 12.9% respectively. The semiconductor sector as a whole is the worst performer in the universe at negative 6.5%, raising questions about order-cycle visibility heading into the second half.

briefs.headline: Oil Markets Steady as US-Iran Diplomatic Effort Holds
briefs.body: Crude prices are little changed after talks between Washington and Tehran concluded in Doha with a focus on the Strait of Hormuz. Gulf oil exports jumped in June on record UAE flows, and Iran is separately reported to be exploring oil sales to Japan while buyers seek extended sanctions relief — a combination that is keeping supply fears contained for now.

briefs.headline: NATO Summit in Ankara Targets Alliance Cohesion
briefs.body: Leaders from across the alliance, including President Trump, have gathered in Ankara where summit language is expected to reaffirm a collective-defence commitment. The meeting comes as officials work to smooth friction between Washington and member states, with key structural challenges to the alliance's posture still unresolved heading into the gathering.

briefs.headline: Iran Funeral Proceedings Begin as Region Watches
briefs.body: Supreme Leader Khamenei is lying in state in Tehran as the country opens a week of official mourning ceremonies. Markets are tracking the geopolitical backdrop closely, with analysts drawing parallels to the 1979 oil shock given the confluence of leadership transition and active sanctions negotiations over Iranian crude exports.

briefs.headline: Bank of England Rate Expectations Shift After Mann Comments
briefs.body: Monetary Policy Committee member Mann indicated that a pullback in rate-hike bets by the market actually strengthens the case for the Bank of England to act. The remarks have drawn attention from fixed-income desks watching the UK policy path, adding a cross-Atlantic dimension to an already complex global rates picture this session.
